summary: This notebook provides an introductory baseline for the Ariel Data Challenge 2025, demonstrating a complete pipeline from raw satellite imaging data to a cross-validated regression model and submission. It shows how to extract 1D time-series signals from 2D FGS1 and AIRS-CH0 sensor frames by differencing even and odd exposures, then derives two physically motivated features representing relative flux reduction during planetary transits. A simple Ridge regression is trained on these features, achieving a high out-of-fold R² score (~0.97) and proving that basic transit photometry features are highly predictive. Readers should learn how to process raw imaging data, construct domain-specific features, and evaluate models using the competition's Gaussian Log Likelihood metric.
competition_flow: Reads raw FGS1 and AIRS-CH0 parquet signal files and metadata, extracts 1D time-series and computes relative flux reduction features, trains a Ridge regression via cross-validation, and generates a submission with mean predictions and uncertainty estimates.
data_reading: Loads metadata via pd.read_csv and pd.read_parquet.
Reads per-planet signal data from nested parquet files using pl.read_parquet and pd.read_parquet.
data_processing: Computes pixel-wise mean over 2D sensor frames (32x32 for FGS1, 32x356 for AIRS).
Calculates net signal by subtracting even-indexed frames from odd-indexed frames to isolate the transit signal.
Flattens the result into 1D time series (67,500 steps for FGS1, 5,625 for AIRS).
features_engineering: Calculates relative flux reduction during transit by averaging flux in predefined in-transit windows and comparing it to out-of-transit baseline windows for both FGS1 and AIRS-CH0 sensors, yielding two scalar features per planet.
insights: Differencing even and odd sensor frames effectively isolates the transit signal from raw imaging data.
Two simple relative flux reduction features capture nearly all predictive variance, achieving an R² of 0.97 with Ridge regression.
The competition metric is a normalized Gaussian Log Likelihood score that penalizes uncertainty calibration as well as point predictions.
critical_findings: All 1100 training planets map to exactly one unique star each, with no repeated observations.
A significant portion of the dataset lacks required calibration files (2200 sets missing).
Flux values exhibit very low variability across the training set (mean ~0.0146, std ~0.0105).
